resume:
Junior Financial Analyst

Summary: Recent finance graduate with one year of experience supporting an FP&A team, eager to grow into a corporate finance role.

Experience:
- Meridian Retail Group — Financial Analyst I (just over a year)
  - Supported the monthly close and helped prepare management reporting decks
  - Built and maintained budget-vs-actual variance trackers in Excel
  - Assisted senior analysts with the annual operating-plan rollup
  - Helped reconcile intercompany balances and flag discrepancies
- Lakeside Bank — Finance Intern (one summer)
  - Shadowed the treasury team and helped tidy cash-position spreadsheets

Skills: Excel (pivot tables, lookups), basic SQL, PowerPoint, variance analysis, NetSuite (basic)
Certifications: CFA Level I candidate
Education: Midtown University, B.S. Finance
jd: Our corporate FP&A team is hiring a Junior Financial Analyst to support monthly close, variance analysis, and management reporting. Reporting to a Senior Analyst, you will own portions of the budget-vs-actual process, help build the quarterly forecast, and prepare slides for the monthly business review. We expect you to be able to walk through a specific variance you investigated — what drove it, how you quantified it, and what you recommended. Strong Excel is required; SQL is a plus. The ideal candidate answers questions directly and grounds their reasoning in specific numbers and concrete examples rather than general statements about finance. This is an in-office role with a clear path to Senior Analyst within two to three years.

history.q: Tell me a bit about your year at Meridian — what does your day-to-day look like?
Which part of the monthly close do you personally own?
Walk me through how you build a budget-vs-actual variance tracker.
Tell me about a specific variance you caught that turned out to matter.
What was the dollar size of that variance?
How did you figure out what was driving it?
Who did you take that finding to, and what did they do with it?
Let's talk about the operating-plan rollup. What was your piece of it?
How long did the rollup take end to end?
Tell me about the intercompany reconciliation work.
What's the trickiest discrepancy you had to chase down?
How comfortable are you in SQL — give me an example query you wrote.
What would you do if the numbers in two reports didn't tie out the day before a board deck was due?
Tell me about a time you had to explain a financial result to a non-finance person.
Why are you looking to leave Meridian?
What do you want to be doing in three years?
Let's go back to that variance you mentioned. I still don't have the number — how big was it in dollars, and over what period?
One more time, just the specifics on that variance: the dollar amount, the time period, and the single biggest driver. Can you give me those three things?

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, so honestly what I love about variance analysis is the storytelling part of it. Like, a number on its own doesn't mean anything — it's about the narrative you build around it for the business. I actually think the most underrated skill in finance is communication, and that's something I've really been working on. I've been reading a lot about data visualization too, and I think dashboards are the future of FP&A. At Meridian we were still really Excel-heavy, which is fine, but I'm excited about tools like Power BI. I'd love to bring more of that modern toolkit to a team.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: what I love about variance analysis is the storytelling part of it

expected-yield: force the candidate off the storytelling/tooling tangent and back onto the three concrete data points already asked for twice — the dollar size of the variance, the time period it spanned, and the single largest driver — testing whether any real quantitative work sits behind the narrative
